just there's two,
Two.

Quite intense. I think.
I don't think I'm gonna ask. You might.
It's because I'm, I'll bring you back. Yes.
Okay.
Alright. Well, uh, good morning
and welcome to the Social Services
and Community Select Committee.
Uh, so this morning we have, uh, ministry
for Disabled People, um, 60 minutes.
And what I might do is ask you to first introduce yourselves
and then make some introductory comments.
Uh, I will also make the observation
that we have interpreters assisting us who are online,
and so they'll ask everyone to just remember that
and, uh, try to, you know, not talk too fast
and allow time, uh, for the interchange of answers
and questions and answers as we go with that.
Um, thank you. Thank
You, Mr. Chair. I was going to
begin by making
that same point about our NZSL interpreters,
the Ministry of Disabled People.
My name's Paula. I'm the Secretary for Disabled People
and Chief Executive of the Ministry.
My signed name is Two Fists,
moving in a forward direction like bicycle,
reflecting my past as a Paralympic cyclist.
Uh, and I'm sitting with colleagues wearing a, uh,
light blue jacket
and I have dark blonde short hair swep to one side.
I'm joined today by my colleagues Ben O Mirror,
deputy Chief Executive Policy and Insights.
Jenny Badley, deputy CE Strategy and Enablement.
Rebecca Alvy, deputy C Outreach and Innovation,
and Elle Baker Katu, chief Advisor Maori.
So today is our birthday.
It's been one year since the ministry became a standalone
public service department, as you've indicated, Mr.
Chair, I'll just spend a few minutes, uh,
outlining some key things
that we have been doing over the past year
before handing over to the committee for questions.
We've been really focused on building a strong foundation
since the machinery of government changes
and shifting to our new structure
and operating model has allowed us to redefine
how we can drive real
and meaningful change for disabled people.
Our new role means looking across sectors
and thinking both short
and long term about the outcomes that matter.
To do this, we are focused on six strategic outcome areas
across accessibility, education, employment, health,
housing, and justice.
These shape our priorities
and work program, which include firstly the refresh
of the New Zealand disability strategy.
We launched tomorrow morning and sets the direction
or disability across government for the next five years.
Last month, we launched the New Zealand Sign Language
Strategy to ensure that NZSL thrives in.
We have also made progress
with other government agencies on implementing the
recommendations that were made to the government
by the United Nations Convention on Rights of Persons
with Disabilities Committee, we've continued
to provide advice on the Royal Commission into abuse
and care response.
To shape the government's response, we have firmed up
and started delivering on our accessibility work program,
including strengthening the alternate format
service across government.
We have invested in initiatives
to create employment opportunities in the public service,
including our recently launched cross agency summer
internship program for disabled students, which saw
13 disabled students placed across 13 government agencies.
And we've continued
to build our ministry to achieve our vision.
We also support cross government priorities
by providing second opinion policy advice,
offering disability expertise,
and brokering opportunities for disabled people
to influence policy.
As part of our stewardship role, we keep an overview of
and contribute to major issues such as transport
and technology developments.
Essential to all of this is the ministry's work alongside
disabled people, t Maori
and their whanau to drive meaningful change
their perspectives, experience and voice are essential.
Everybody in ROA has a role in improving the
lives of disabled people.
And so we will continue
to build relationships across central
and local government, business and industry
and the community to make change happen.
Our ability to deliver our work rests on a strong
and well performing ministry.
While disabled people still face many barriers,
we are now in an excellent, strong position
to accelerate progress.
Our vision remains clear, disabled people
thriving in New Zealand.
I'm happy to take your questions. Thank you.
Um, first question. Thank you.
Um, thank you for that overview
and, um, the work that you're doing.
Um, I've got a question
around the employment side of things.
You've mentioned the initiatives within the public service
seen that in the annual report as well.
Um, you will also know that the unemployment figure,
the June quarter shows the unemployment figure
for disabled people is 14.1% the highest it's ever been
since it was recorded in 2017.
Um, you would've also heard from disabled people
that many are struggling to put food on the table
and to, um, access the basic necessities of life.
What employment initiatives are underway that are new that,
uh, f Kaha is supporting
or investing in, um, to shift those figures?
Your performance measures are just
around the employment rate going up under utilization going
down and the pay gap going down.
Um, but what are the initiatives that will get us there?
Thank you for that question.
So, um, there are a number of things that we are doing.
Um, so you are right.
The employment gap between disabled people
and non-disabled people in New Zealand is too wide,
and that's due to a range of things.
It's around lack of reasonable accommodation
and supports for disabled people.
It can be related to transport and wider infrastructure.
Um, we know that disabled people are less likely
to have a post post school qualification,
and we know that there continue to be attitude.
So those are some of the barriers to employment.
The things that we are doing are as follows.
Firstly, uh,
members will see tomorrow when the New Zealand disability
Strategy is released, that there are a number of actions
that ministers have agreed to around employment, uh, which
we are confident will help with narrowing that gap.
Um, members may have seen some
of those draft actions in the strategy.
Uh, the final strategy comes out tomorrow.
I touched briefly on the internship program
across the public service.
That is really important
because the public service has an opportunity
to be a real role model in this space.
This is the first time that we have gone across government
with such an internship program with 10 agencies, um,
taking disabled young people
and giving them a foot in the door.
Some of those people had not worked for years
and were just looking for that opportunity.
The next thing that we will be launching in the first part
of next calendar year is our employment website.
So what we know from our engagement,
both in the public sector
and private sector, is
that actually most employers want to do the right thing.
Where some of the difficulties arise is in having access
to good information
and good advice in a good suite of policies.
So we are responding to that
by launching an employment website in the first part
of next year, and we'll be doing a lot of, um,
promotional activity around that.
And that's intended to be a, a, um, support base
for employers.
We are also, um, working much more closely
with the private sector than we've had the opportunity
to do in the past.
And so working with organizations, um,
to really lift that number.
We've also started working with the Institute of Directors
and shaping up a work program
around governance and other opportunities.
So there is a lot to be done in the, uh,
New Zealand disability strategy.
You will see that there are a range of, um, indicators
to measure success over the next five years.
So those are the things that, uh,
we are doing in leading most of that work, uh, as well
as contributing to other government initiatives.
Um, are you aware that organizations
that have a government contract to place
disabled people into work have had a change in their
contract obligations such that they can only have 10%
of their clientele who are non beneficiaries,
thus impacting a number of people
who may not receive a government benefit
and want work who cannot be placed into work?
I'm not aware of that specific change,
but we are really focused on closing
that employment gap between disabled people
and non-disabled people.
The trend has been, uh, that gap has
trended the same for a very long time now,
and it is really important.
Our minister holds the employment portfolio as well
as disability, and it's an area of, um,
great interest to her.
It's great to see you all this morning.
Thank you for being here.
So, um, wanting to know what assurances you can provide
that f Kaha is genuinely making progress on its core mandate
to drive meaningful change for disabled people
when the ministry's current performance measures focused
largely on internal policy advice quality
and do not yet include clear measurable indicators,
especially for outcomes
that rely on coordinated actions across other government
agencies, for example, housing, education
and, um, and jobs.
Thank you for that question. Um, you are right.
There are only two performance measures, uh,
when a new agency is a is established members maybe
where you have a relatively short period of time
to develop your statement of strategic contentions.
And we didn't have sufficient time to develop longer term,
uh, indicators, cases.
The, um, so the two that are,
that we've reported on in our annual report, um,
next year we will be reporting on more performance measures.
Some of the things to bear in mind, um, around
demonstrating that outcomes for disabled people, uh,
hopefully improve, uh, work that, um,
supports the outcomes framework
for the New Zealand disability strategy.
So what you'll see tomorrow is a very clear outcomes
framework that shows what the current data indicates
and then where we expect that
to go over the next five years.
So that's really important
and it will be very clear when the implementation plan comes
out next calendar year,
which agencies are responsible for that.
And VA Kaha has a key role in leading
or contributing to that.
The second, um, set of measures that
I think are important to be aware of is
I talked briefly about the recommendations
that the government is implementing that the UN recommended
to New Zealand in 2022.
Those actions all sit on the human rights monitor,
that the Ministry of Justice, um, administers,
and people can go into that
and will be updated again early next year with
where those different actions are at.
And that's an important contribution in terms
of measuring better outcomes.
Just a couple of other very specific things
that we, we are doing.
Um, we have worked really hard to improve the quality
of data and evidence around disabled people in New Zealand.
And one of the things we've done is join the Social
Investment agency mahi tahi data
and analytics platform so that we can start
to have interactive dashboards
and share the progress of disabled people in New Zealand.
And we also fund research, um, that is, uh, undertaken
by the Donald Beasley, um, institute
who look at particular areas
of outcomes for disabled people.
Mm-hmm. So the long
and short of that, um, is this is a moment in time
where we only have those two performance measures.
They will increase in our annual reporting next year,
as well as all
of the other more external reporting things that I touched on.
Oh, thank you so much. It's great to hear that
that's coming and we look forward to seeing it.
So with those, um, the strategy coming out tomorrow
and those expected, um, things to be published next year,
we can expect to see that whole
of government performance framework
that clearly links fa kaha work
and the work of partner agencies
to measurable improvements in the lives
of disabled people in their whanau.
Fantastic. Um, I've just got a couple more subs.
So what steps are being taken to encourage agencies
to work in accordance with enabling good lives principles
and that approach for across government?
So in our strategic documents, we talk about
being guided by, uh, t
and also the UN Convention on the Rights of Persons
with disabilities and the principles of enabling good lives.
And last year the government, um, uh,
expressed its continued support for the principles
of enabling good lives.
We, um, meet with the National Enabling Good Lives group.
We are just right now in the process of agreeing, um, a
training program for our staff so that, um, we can make sure
that when we are working across government, we
can talk about enabling good lives principles
to the same extent as we can the UN CRPD
in our policy work across government.
So, um, in the period under review, we've
provided advice on a significant number of, um, policies
that agencies, um, have consulted with us on.
That's, you know, part of our key role
and we often talk about the principles
of enabling good lives, um, the eight principles
and the importance of those principles
and the lives of disabled people.
Thank you. I've Just got, I'm gonna stop on that. Sure.
Um, will, you mentioned meeting with the NGL group.
Um, is it true that they're going to be downgraded
and will no longer be a ministerial advisory group?
I,
We are working through
with the National Enabling Good Lives Group at the moment,
uh, what our relationship agreement looks like with them.
What's the timeframe on that?
Um, we are working through, in the short term,
getting the training program established
and then looking longer term what other key pieces
of work we can do together.
But one of the things that I'm really conscious of, um, is
the number of disabled peoples organizations
and representatives groups, um, family groups who want
to engage with vaha
and we work really hard to make sure
that we have good relationships and agreements in place.
So you can't rule out that they won't be downgraded
and will no longer be a ministerial advisory group.
That's a decision for the minister. Thank
You.

relationship into play as much as it goes.

And so we'll be drawing on that information as well
as survey information, um,
to report against the New Zealand disability strategy.
We also work with the social investment agency generally
around, um, what kinds of initiatives
and longer term sort of planning can we think about so
that we are supporting disabled people early
for better longer term outcomes.
Thank you. You a supplementary.
Thank you. Um, Yeah,

Disability. Yeah.
Yeah. So, um, the short answer is yes, we'll be working to
refine and provide more granular information.
The extent to which we can do that
critically depends on the quality of administrative data
that is available across governments.
And so that's why we are also putting quite a lot

and disability and data evidence
and working group to lift the quality of data, um,
available across government to,
to better understand outcomes for disabled people.
And we also rely on general social, um,
or general survey, sorry, information.
So what we are really pleased to see now,

So we are getting a much richer picture from survey data

granular set of measures.
So that's our intention rather than a up or down.
So our work within with, um, the social investment agency
has a particular focus on identifying, uh, markers
for disability within administrative data so
that we can do more granular, granular
and detailed analysis, um, based on particular groups.
So it might be learning disabled, it might be people, uh,
who have sensory disabilities, um,
those sorts of pieces of work.
And we need to develop, uh, what are described
as code modules to enable that more detailed work to happen.
Thank you. Um, another supplemental on
that point actually, because just, uh, visiting a lot
of my schools, you know, personally across my, um,
across my region there,
there's obviously a very significant cohort of students
who have a variety of, uh, learning disabilities.
Mm-hmm. And one of the challenges is
certainly identification.
The other one is the appropriate resources, um,
to assist with that.
Uh, our is is some of your policy works,
obviously there's crossover, probably social investment
to some degree, um, ministry of Education.
How much, how much of that work is sort of sits within your

and how much is that a relationship

Yeah, we've, we've got a really, um, strong relationship
with the Ministry of Education.
Um, the Ministry of Education,
we're involved in the New Zealand disability Strategy

and the community and,

to lift, um, uh, opportunities
for disabled young people in education.
Key to that as well is lifting performance

And so we have been working really hard with the Ministry
of Education on that.
Thank you. And your question, Paula,
Um, uh, thank you firstly for all the work that you do
and, and I'm Marie, um, I'm eager to see, uh,
progress going forward, but just going back to, uh,
the restructure and the focus on, on change processes
and, um, could I just ask quickly how

Um, and then following that, um, would there have been,
would that have been a reason for underspend, uh, on some
of your work programs and how did that, um, all work out?
Yeah, thank you for that question.
So the short answer is yes, we did have an underspend,
um, of 4.38 million.
Um, the significant portion of that funding
was funding given to us to establish the ministry.
Um, so around accessible properties technology, for example,
um, we do have approval to transfer
that into the current financial year.
And so we have a, a particular project aimed at,
um, spending that money.
So the underspend is largely, uh, it is due

Uh, we have a very comprehensive
work program across the six areas that I touched on, um,
to make the greatest use of,
of those public funds this year.
Yeah.

it's reflected in the report,

to, um, honor our obligations under which, uh,
as an agency, you know, like we,
I take my responsibilities really seriously.
Key to that has been building a broader set
of relationships.

We have also, um, broadened our relationship

We support the TUR Maori Advisory Group for

And so I was very keen to ensure that

We also, this year, invested in a kaiga, um,

um, we are also doing a, a lot of work, um,
through Elle's great leadership around, uh, working to
increase the ministry's capability around, um,
Ang and taking a Maori, um, approach to our work.

or making sure that we are bringing

Quick one, if I may.

source_metadata.title: 2025/12/01 - SSC (Part 2)
source_metadata.description:
Monday 1 December 2025 | Social Services and Community Committee

2024/25 Annual review of the Ministry of Disabled People

This video includes live interpreting. The goal is to provide clear and accurate information, but some details may be missed or contain errors. It is shared to give you access to the information, but please double-check any details before relying on them.

Hearing of evidence:

Ministry for Disabled People – Whaikaha

Paula Tesoriero, Chief Executive
Ginny Baddeley, Deputy Chief Executive Strategy and Enablement
Ben O’Meara, Deputy Chief Executive Policy and Insights
Rebecca Elvy, Deputy Chief Executive Outreach and Innovation
Ronelle Baker, Kaihautū – Chief Advisor Māori
